skill.name: pdf
skill.description: Local PDF processing skill for extracting text and tables, rendering pages, creating PDFs, merging or splitting files, and filling PDF forms.
skill.summary: Process, inspect, create, and fill PDF documents with local tools and scripts.
skill.author: DYAI2025

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review found most blocker and network matches are false positives from Markdown examples, PDF terminology, license links, and tests. Real risk remains because the skill directs assistants to run local PDF-processing commands and helper scripts that parse untrusted PDFs and write output files.

medium_findings.title: Local Helper Scripts Parse Untrusted PDFs
medium_findings.description: The skill includes Python helpers that open user-supplied PDFs with pypdf and pdf2image. This is expected for the skill, but malformed or very large PDFs can trigger parser bugs, memory pressure, or long-running conversions.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code directly loads user-provided PDF paths and performs rendering or parsing. The behavior is legitimate for a PDF skill, but it is a real exposure to untrusted document processing risk.

medium_findings.title: User-Controlled Output Paths Write Files Locally
medium_findings.description: Several scripts write generated PDFs, JSON files, or images to paths supplied on the command line. This can overwrite files if the assistant or user provides an unsafe output path.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file writes are clear and use command-line paths without path allowlisting. No malicious intent is present, but local overwrite risk is real.

medium_findings.title: Workflow Encourages External Local Commands
medium_findings.description: The skill documentation instructs the assistant to run Python helper scripts and command-line PDF tools such as pdftotext, qpdf, and pdftk. These commands are normal for PDF automation, but marketplace users should understand that the skill relies on local execution.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The evidence is explicit in the markdown instructions. The commands are local PDF utilities rather than suspicious shell payloads, so the concern is operational risk rather than malware.

low_findings.title: Static Blocker Matches Are Mostly False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported weak cryptography and C2 keyword matches occur in PDF examples, field names, validation text, tests, and ordinary documentation. Review found no confirmed malware command-and-control behavior or weak cryptographic implementation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Multiple sampled high-risk locations resolve to benign PDF labels, examples, or test assertions. I did not find evidence of cryptographic code or C2 communication.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Documentation Links
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL findings are license terms or source documentation references. The reviewed code does not make network requests to these URLs.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs appear in comments and license text only. No evidence found of active HTTP clients, credential upload, or data exfiltration.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Triggered External Command Detector
low_findings.description: Many external-command findings are Markdown code fences or inline command examples, not Ruby backtick execution. Some examples do describe local commands, but the detector label itself is inaccurate.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The sampled hits are inside Markdown fences or inline examples. They are not Ruby shell execution syntax in runnable Ruby code.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local Script Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill depends on running bundled Python scripts from the local filesystem. This is functional but should be disclosed because script execution changes the host environment through generated files.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The instruction text directly tells the assistant to run local scripts. The scripts are relevant to the skill and not hidden.

dangerous_patterns.title: Filesystem Writes From User-Supplied Paths
dangerous_patterns.description: Helper scripts create output images, JSON files, and PDFs using command-line paths. Unsafe path choices can overwrite local files.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The write calls are explicit and accept path arguments. This is a bounded local risk, not evidence of hostile behavior.

content.value_statement: PDF workflows often require several tools for extraction, conversion, merging, and forms. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through local scripts and proven PDF libraries.
actual_capabilities: Extract text, metadata, tables, and images from PDF documents.
Create new PDFs with Python examples using pypdf and reportlab.
Merge, split, rotate, crop, encrypt, decrypt, and watermark PDFs.
Detect fillable form fields and prepare field-value mappings.
Render PDF pages to images for visual inspection and OCR workflows.
Fill non-fillable forms by placing text annotations at validated boxes.
limitations: Requires local Python packages and command-line PDF tools to be installed.
Does not guarantee accurate OCR for low-quality scanned documents.
Form filling may need manual visual validation before final output.
Processing very large or malformed PDFs can be slow or fail.

use_cases.title: Fill government or business forms
use_cases.description: Use field detection, validation images, and annotations to complete structured or scanned PDF forms.

use_cases.title: Extract document data for review
use_cases.description: Pull text, tables, metadata, and page images from PDF reports for comparison and analysis.

use_cases.title: Automate PDF document assembly
use_cases.description: Create repeatable workflows for merging, splitting, rotating, watermarking, and generating PDFs.

prompt_templates.title: Extract text from a PDF
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the PDF skill to extract readable text from this PDF. Summarize the main sections and note pages with missing or unclear text.

prompt_templates.title: Merge and organize files
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the PDF skill to merge these PDFs in the order I provide. Add a short checklist of the resulting page order before creating the output.

prompt_templates.title: Fill a PDF form
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the PDF skill to determine whether this form has fillable fields. Fill it with the supplied values and verify the completed PDF visually.

prompt_templates.title: Build a batch extraction workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the PDF skill to design a local workflow that extracts text and tables from every PDF in this folder and reports failures separately.

output_examples.input: A five-page vendor invoice PDF with item tables and payment terms.
output_examples.output: Extracted invoice summary with vendor name, invoice date, totals, table rows, and pages where values were found.

output_examples.input: A blank registration PDF and a list of applicant details.
output_examples.output: Completed PDF form plus a validation note describing fields filled, skipped fields, and any values needing review.

output_examples.input: Three policy PDFs that must become one review packet.
output_examples.output: Merged packet with confirmed source order, page counts, and a note about any rotation or layout adjustments.

best_practices: Work on copies of original PDFs and write outputs to a dedicated folder.
Inspect generated images or final PDFs before using filled forms externally.
Install only the PDF libraries and command-line tools required for the task.
anti_patterns: Do not run the scripts on unknown PDFs without resource limits or supervision.
Do not overwrite source PDFs with generated outputs.
Do not assume OCR or visual field detection is correct without review.

faq.question: Can this skill fill interactive PDF forms?
faq.answer: Yes. It can inspect fillable fields, map valid values, and write a filled output PDF.

faq.question: Can it handle PDFs without fillable fields?
faq.answer: Yes. It can render pages to images and add text annotations at validated locations.

faq.question: Does this skill need internet access?
faq.answer: No active network access is required by the reviewed scripts. Some documentation contains reference links.

faq.question: Which tools does it rely on?
faq.answer: It uses Python libraries such as pypdf, pdfplumber, reportlab, pdf2image, PIL, and optional command-line PDF tools.

faq.question: Is it safe for sensitive documents?
faq.answer: It processes files locally, but users should manage permissions, output paths, and retention for sensitive PDFs.

faq.question: Can it extract tables from scanned PDFs?
faq.answer: Scanned PDFs may need OCR first. Table accuracy depends on scan quality and page layout.
